details: The scheme “S.P. Nautiyal Sangrahalaya” implemented by the Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology, offers a deep insight into the Great Himalayas. The museum showcases geo-scientific maps, charts, specimens, models, and various educational and awareness-generating exhibits. Samples of rocks, minerals, and fossils found throughout the Himalayan region are also displayed here. This museum serves as a valuable resource for understanding the geology and natural history of the Himalayas.
benefits: Provides in-depth knowledge and understanding of the Great Himalayas through geo-scientific exhibits.
Raises awareness about the geological and natural heritage of the Himalayan region.
Offers a wide range of exhibits including maps, charts, models, and actual samples of rocks, minerals, and fossils.
Open to both national and international visitors, making it a globally accessible resource for Himalayan studies.
eligibility: The applicant can be any national/ international visitor, or a large group/ an institution seeking a guided tour.

offline: If an individual or a family wishes to visit the Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology Museum, they can visit on any working day (Monday to Friday, from 9:30 AM to 5:30 PM). It is advisable to obtain prior permission by sending an email to rksehgal@wihg.res.in or by calling 0135-2525263 / 118.
For guided tours for large groups or institutional visits, it is mandatory to obtain prior permission from the Director of the Institution: director@wihg.res.in

faqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the "S.P. Nautiyal Museum" ?
faqs.answer: The "S.P. Nautiyal Museum" primarily aims to provide deep insights into the Great Himalayas through various geo-scientific and educational exhibits.

faqs.question: What types of exhibits can visitors expect to see in the museum?
faqs.answer: Visitors can expect to see geo-scientific maps, charts, specimens, models, and various educational displays, including samples of rocks, minerals, and fossils from the entire Himalayan region.

faqs.question: Who is eligible to visit the S.P. Nautiyal Museum?
faqs.answer: All national and international visitors are eligible to visit the S.P. Nautiyal Museum.

faqs.question: Are there any fees to enter the museum?
faqs.answer: No, entry to the S.P. Nautiyal Museum is free for all visitors.

faqs.question: What are the operating hours for individual and family visitors?
faqs.answer: Individual and family visitors can visit the museum on any working day, from Monday to Friday, between 9:30 AM and 5:30 PM.

faqs.question: Is it necessary for individuals or families to obtain prior permission before visiting?
faqs.answer: It is advisable for individuals or families to obtain prior permission before visiting, which can be done via email or phone.

faqs.question: How can individuals or families contact the museum for prior permission?
faqs.answer: They can obtain prior permission by sending an email to rksehgal@wihg.res.in or by calling 0135-2525263/ 118.

faqs.question: What is the procedure for large groups or institutions to arrange a guided tour?
faqs.answer: For large groups or institutional tours, it is mandatory to obtain prior permission from the Director of the Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology.

faqs.question: Whom should large groups or institutions contact for permission for a guided tour?
faqs.answer: Large groups or institutions should contact the Director at director@wihg.res.in to obtain prior permission for guided tours. director@wihg.res.in

faqs.question: Does the museum offer insights into the geological history of the Himalayas?
faqs.answer: Yes, the museum offers deep insights into the Great Himalayas, including its geological history, through its geo-scientific exhibits.

faqs.question: Is the museum accessible to international tourists?
faqs.answer: Yes, the museum is accessible to all national and international visitors.

faqs.question: Does the museum display actual samples of rocks, minerals, and fossils?
faqs.answer: Yes, the museum displays samples of rocks, minerals, and fossils that are found throughout the Himalayan region.
